concept: R7 Autograd-IX architectural decisions locked in during Week 1
content:
# R7 Autograd-IX — locked-in decisions

IX Week 1 R7 shipped `crates/ix-autograd` from scratch over commits dd5cc5b → 08237c7. These decisions are NOT to be re-litigated in subsequent weeks without explicit justification; they were made with multi-provider code review (Codex + Claude) and validated by 22 passing finite-diff tests at 1e-5 tolerance.

## Decision 1 — Scratch over ndarray, NOT candle/burn/dfdx

Codex code review called this correctly on Day 0. Reasoning: the 52-crate IX workspace is already ndarray-native. Every boundary crossing to candle::Tensor would cost ownership/shape/device reconciliation dominating the actual autograd work. For a 1-week prototype wrapping 5-10 tools, the boundary cost exceeds the tape implementation cost.

The `TensorData` enum leaves an explicit future door:
```rust
pub enum TensorData {
    F64(ndarray::ArrayD<f64>),
    // Future: Candle(candle_core::Tensor), Gpu(ix_gpu::Buffer), ...
}
```

If ix-autograd ever needs GPU backends, the door is open without breaking downstream tool implementations.

## Decision 2 — ExecutionMode has 4 variants (not Codex's 3)

```rust
pub enum ExecutionMode {
    Eager,             // current ix-pipeline behavior, no tape
    Train,             // all tools diff, tape built, gradients flow
    Mixed,             // diff subgraph on tape, non-diff upstream frozen as constants
    VerifyFiniteDiff,  // CI gate: verify analytical grads against finite diffs
}
```

Codex's original proposal was Eager/Train/VerifyFiniteDiff. Claude's code review added **Mixed** as essential for gradual migration: every real pipeline during the R7 rollout will have differentiable and non-differentiable tools coexisting, and Mixed is the mode that makes this work by freezing non-diff tool outputs as constants at the graph boundary.

## Decision 3 — Tool state is typed `Box<dyn Any + Send + Sync>`, NOT `serde_json::Value`

Day 2 shipped tool state as `HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>`. Day 2 review (r7-day2-review.md §3.2) flagged this as fragile — JSON round-trips for integer handles, typos silently return None, no compile-time safety. Day 3 replaced it with:

```rust
pub fn set_tool_state<T: Any + Send + Sync>(&mut self, key: &str, value: T)
pub fn get_tool_state<T: Any + Send + Sync>(&self, key: &str) -> Option<&T>
pub fn take_tool_state<T: Any + Send + Sync>(&mut self, key: &str) -> Option<T>
```

The refactor took 25 minutes (budgeted 2 hours) and eliminated an entire class of runtime errors. Future tools should use typed state structs, not JSON.

## Decision 4 — FFT backward correction for ix-signal

The Codex review reference implementation assumed a true half-spectrum `rfft` (numpy/JAX convention returning N/2+1 bins). `ix_signal::fft::rfft` is a thin wrapper around the full complex FFT, returning all N bins. Consequence: the Hermitian-mirror rule from the Codex review does NOT apply — the spectrum already contains both halves explicitly.

The backward formula for `f(x) = g(|FFT(x)|)` with real input and ix-signal's 1/N normalization on ifft is:

```
dL/dx[n] = N * Re( ifft(grad_y)[n] )
```

The N factor undoes the 1/N normalization. Empirically verified by finite diff in `verify_rfft_magnitude_backward` at tolerance 1e-4. If ix-signal ever grows a true half-spectrum rfft, the Hermitian-mirror rule (with DC and Nyquist self-conjugate edge cases) comes back — full reference implementation in r7-autograd-codex-review.md.

## Decision 5 — Reverse walker uses string-match dispatch

```rust
let input_grads = match op {
    "input" => continue,
    "add" => backward_add(self, &node_clone, &grad_out)?,
    "sub" => backward_sub(self, &node_clone, &grad_out)?,
    "mul" => backward_mul(self, &node_clone, &grad_out)?,
    ...
    #[cfg(feature = "fft-autograd")]
    "rfft_magnitude" => crate::ops_fft::backward_rfft_magnitude(...)?,
    other => return Err(AutogradError::Numerical(format!("unknown op `{other}`"))),
};
```

The Day 2 review recommended migrating to an `OpKind` enum at 6+ ops. We're at 9 ops now; enum migration is queued for R7 Week 2. Defer until the match exceeds ~12 arms or a performance measurement shows dispatch overhead.

## Go/no-go gate result

Week 1 gate required ≥20× fewer gradient evals than ix-evolution GA. Measured: **31 Adam steps** vs 5000-10000 GA reference = **242× speedup**. Wall-clock: 13.6 µs/Adam step via criterion benchmarks. PASS on every sub-criterion; 2-month full R7 implementation commit is justified.
